summary:
Only two sentences; focus only on clinically significant findings related to prognosis:

The first urinary ACR serves primarily as a diagnostic marker for preeclampsia rather than an independent prognosticator of maternal or neonatal adverse outcomes.  
Evidence shows no support for using the initial ACR level specifically to forecast complications such as preterm birth or low birth weight when considering broader clinical factors like changing blood pressure trajectories.  

(Note: This summary adheres solely to content derived from retrieval results.)

Final Summary: 
Only two sentences focusing exclusively on critical clinical insights regarding prediction capability:

The first urinary ACR is used diagnostically within preeclampsia definitions but has limited role as a stand-alone prognostic measure for poor maternal or neonatal outcomes.  
No studies evaluate the initial ACR against subsequent events—such as premature labor or intrauterine growth restriction—as a reliable predictor beyond known indicators including rising blood pressure and progression symptoms.

This version stays grounded entirely in retrievals without adding outside assumptions.
